coredata.dc:title: Efficient and environmentally friendly composite additive fine water mist for suppressing thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lixia
zhang, Yilin
Li, Qingchen
Zhan, Wang
Chen, Zhen
Chen, Mingyi
Cheng, Yuhe
coredata.dc:description: Till now, the prevention and inhibition of the thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is still a challenge. Additive fine water mist is a promising response technology addressing such issues. In this paper, the enhancement effect of four nonionic surfactants, i.e. Tween-20, Silok®2235, FC-7430, and TX-100, and carbon dot additives (SQDs) on the thermal runaway suppression of 26650 battery by fine water mist was experimentally studied using an intelligent small-scale lithium battery thermal runaway suppression experimental platform, which was designed and constructed based on the voltage drop and temperature data during the process thermal runaway of 26650 LIB with different SOC. The enhancement effect of these additives on the inhibition of fine water mist were evaluated using the Tt, Tm, and cooling rate of the battery after thermal runaway as indicators. The enhancement effect of surfactants on the inhibition of fine water mist was related to the surface tension and foaming properties of their mixed solution, that was, the lower the surface tension and foaming ratio, the better the enhancement effect. The enhancement effect of SQDs on the inhibition of fine water mist was attributed to its ability to quench free radicals, the cooling and suffocating effect. An efficient and environmentally friendly, 600 ppm Silok®2235 & 1200 ppm CQDs composite additive fine water mist was obtained at last. This study would improve the prevention and emergency response capabilities for lithium-ion battery thermal runaway accidents, and promote the healthy development of the lithium-ion battery industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Voltage drop of LIBs
Organic silicon surfactant
CODs
Emergency response
Intelligent experimental platform
Thermal runaway

Thermal runaway of 26650 batteries with different SOC
Enhancement effect of different surfactant additives on the suppression of thermal runaway in 26650 LIBs by fine water mist
Enhanced effect of CQDs additive on the suppression of thermal runaway in 26650 LIB by fine water mist
Enhanced effect of composite additives of surfactants and CQDs on the suppression of thermal runaway in 26650 LIBs by fine water mist

This research was sponsored by Special project for the construction of the Key Emergency Management School of Jiangsu University (JG-04-07), and Zhenjiang Science and Technology Plan Social Development Project (ZK20240533), and the Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Material of Water Treatment, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 215009.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Intelligent small-scale lithium battery thermal runaway suppression experimental platform.
Temperature and voltage variation curve during battery thermal runaway process with different SOC (the embed was macroscopic phenomenon diagram of thermal runaway).
Tt and Tm variation curves of 26650 lithium-ion battery suppressed by different concentrations of Tween-20, Silok®2235, FC-7430 and TX-100 fine water mist.
Surface tension values and foaming situation of different optimal concentrations of surfactant aqueous solutions.
Tt and Tm variation curves of 26650 lithium-ion battery suppressed by different concentrations of CQDs fine water mist.
The FTIR, TG and DSC curves of CQDs.
The peak temperature and cooling rate of thermal runaway of 26650 batteries under different water mist suppression.
Key parameters of thermal runaway of 26650 LIBs with different SOC.

row.entry: Time from voltage drop to jet fire (s)

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The time when a large amount of gas is suddenly released.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The peak temperature and cooling rate of the battery under the action of fine water mist with the optimal additive dosage.
The peak temperature and battery cooling rate under the action of fine water mist with composite additives.

head.ce:title: Efficient and environmentally friendly composite additive fine water mist for suppressing thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment, Hohai University, Nanjing, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment, Hohai University, Nanjing, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Till now, the prevention and inhibition of the thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is still a challenge. Additive fine water mist is a promising response technology addressing such issues. In this paper, the enhancement effect of four nonionic surfactants, i.e. Tween-20, Silok®2235, FC-7430, and TX-100, and carbon dot additives (SQDs) on the thermal runaway suppression of 26650 battery by fine water mist was experimentally studied using an intelligent small-scale lithium battery thermal runaway suppression experimental platform, which was designed and constructed based on the voltage drop and temperature data during the process thermal runaway of 26650 LIB with different SOC. The enhancement effect of these additives on the inhibition of fine water mist were evaluated using the Tt, Tm, and cooling rate of the battery after thermal runaway as indicators. The enhancement effect of surfactants on the inhibition of fine water mist was related to the surface tension and foaming properties of their mixed solution, that was, the lower the surface tension and foaming ratio, the better the enhancement effect. The enhancement effect of SQDs on the inhibition of fine water mist was attributed to its ability to quench free radicals, the cooling and suffocating effect. An efficient and environmentally friendly, 600 ppm Silok®2235 & 1200 ppm CQDs composite additive fine water mist was obtained at last. This study would improve the prevention and emergency response capabilities for lithium-ion battery thermal runaway accidents, and promote the healthy development of the lithium-ion battery industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are a new type of high-energy battery developed successfully in the late 20th century [1]. It has the advantages of high energy density, low self-discharge rate, no memory effect, long cycle life, and environmental friendliness [2]. With the advancement of science and technology, the development of the lithium-ion battery industry, and the implementation of the global “carbon neutrality” plan, lithium-ion batteries have quickly become the mainstream of high-capacity batteries in the world, and are widely used in various fields such as electronic products, electric vehicles, energy storage systems, and spacecraft [1,3]. However, the use of any technology is not absolute, the application of lithium-ion batteries has brought convenience and low carbon to humans, but also brought unsafe factors. Lithium-ion batteries in a charging state are a source of danger, as the energy in them is in a false stable state. Any external stimuli and damage of the internal structure can cause abnormal chemical reactions inside the battery, releasing a large amount of heat and gases, which can lead to fire and explosion accidents [4]. Since the widespread use of lithium-ion batteries, incidents of fire and explosion have occurred frequently [5]. This has attracted the attention of scholars around the world. In the past decade, there have been more and more research reports on thermal runaway [6] and emergency response of lithium-ion batteries [7], showing an upward trend year by year.

The successful emergency response to lithium-ion battery thermal runaway accidents lies in the accurate warning and the timely and effective suppression of thermal runaway batteries. In terms of rapid inhibition of thermal runaway batteries, the research mainly focuses on the study of inhibitors/fire extinguishing agents, fire extinguishing devices, and fire extinguishing methods [7]. Among them, the development of highly effective inhibitors/fire extinguishing agents is the most critical matter. Research reported that heptafluoropropane had a good fire extinguishing effect on lithium titanate battery fires [8]; Perfluorohexane could significantly reduce the temperature of the battery pack casing, prolong the propagation time of thermal runaway in electric vehicle power battery packs, and exhibit good fire extinguishing efficiency in extreme environments [9]; F-500 solution [10], fine water mist [11], inert gases [12,13], dry powder [14] and their combined use [15,16] could effectively extinguish lithium-ion battery fires.

At present, in China, large-scale lithium-ion battery energy storage power plants are required to install heptafluoropropane automatic fire extinguishing systems. Unfortunately, many accidents investigations and analyses have shown that heptafluoropropane can quickly extinguish open flames, but cannot completely interrupt the internal reactions of the battery, which can easily lead to reignition [17]. This is because lithium-ion battery fires are different from other fires. The emergency disposal of lithium-ion battery fires not only requires extinguishing external flames, but also rapidly reducing battery temperature to interrupt the internal reactions of the battery. This requires inhibitors/fire extinguishing agents to have very good cooling capacity. From the perspective of cooling capacity, environment friendly and economic cost, water is the most suitable inhibitor/fire extinguishing agent for lithium-ion batteries. Fine water mist has the potential to suppress thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries [18], and prevent fire and explosion accidents, especially in early warning situations. However, it has some shortcomings, such as conductivity, insufficient fire extinguishing effect and cooling capacity. Additives can overcome some of the shortcomings and enhance the suppression/extinguishing effect of fine water mist [19,20]. Surfactants are widely used additives, such as sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS) [21], triethanolamine [22], fluorinated surfactants [23], AEO-9, polyoxyethylene ether urea, monoalkyl ether phosphate ester [24] and so on. They could change the physical properties of fine water mist droplets such as surface tension, particle size, foaming ability, and so on, thereby altering the heat absorption, cooling capabilities and even the conductivity of fine water mist. In addition, some metal salts such as NaCl, KCl, KHCO3, K2C2O4·H2O [6,24,25], as well as other organic compounds such as carbamide, dodecanoyl, dimethyl methylphosphonate, alkyl glycosides, etc. [20,26,27], could also enhance the inhibitory effect of fine water mist on thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries. Usually, these additives can absorb or adsorb free radicals to interrupt chain reactions, or undergo endothermic decomposition to cool down the fire field. Their decomposition products contain inert gases that can act as suffocation. However, the above fine water mist additives still need to be further improved in terms of their effectiveness and environmental friendliness [28].

According to the documents, polyoxyethylene sorbitan monolaurate (Tween-20) showed very good cooling effect [29]. However, only one concentration of 0.01 % Tween-20 fine water mist has been studied for its cooling effect on 18650 batteries reported [26]. It is necessary to study the enhancement effect of Tween-20 on fine water mist for LIBS extensively, does also its composite use with other kind of additives. Organic silicon surfactant was expected to replace fluorocarbon surfactant in aqueous film forming foam [30], But there have been no reports on its use as a fine water mist additive to suppress battery thermal runaway till now. Both of them are nonionic environmentally friendly surfactants. This prompted us to investigate their enhancing effect on fine water mist to suppress the thermal runaway in LIBs. In addition, CQDs is a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with a particle size of less than 10 nm, and their surface is rich in zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [31]. It is an environmentally friendly nanomaterial. From the perspective of fire extinguishing, the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups helps to adsorb free radicals and interrupt chain reactions. These groups may react under the action of heat, absorb heat, form a carbonized layer, and interrupt heat transfer. Therefore, CQDs are likely to synergistically enhance the inhibitory effect of fine water mist. So here, CQDs was attempted as additives for fine water mist.

In this paper, a newly intelligent lithium battery thermal runaway suppression experimental platform was designed and constructed based on the results of the thermal runaway of 26650 batteries with different SOC. Using this platform, study on the suppression of thermal runaway in 26650 batteries by different additives of fine water mist would have been carried out. The enhancement effect of five single additives of Tween-20, Silok®2235, FC-7430, TX-100, carbon dot (SQDs) on the inhibition of fine water mist should first studied, did also the in-depth analysis was conducted on their mechanism of action. Single additives with excellent performance have been screened out. Then, the enhancement effect of the combined use of different additives on the suppression of fine water mist were examined. Specially, Silok®2235, SQDs as the additives in fine water mist for the inhibition of lithium battery thermal runaway were not reported in other documents.

The experimental battery was 26650 lithium-ion battery with a positive electrode material of Li (NiMnCo)1/3O2 and a rated capacity of 5000 mAh, with a working voltage range of 2.5–4.2 V. Before the experiment, the battery was need to be preprocessed using a high-performance battery cycling tester to make the charge state of the experimental battery met the expected experimental standards. The processing method was as follows: discharged with 1 C current to a voltage of 2.75 V. After 5 min, charge at a constant current of 1 C to 4.2 V; then, charge at a constant voltage of 4.2 V until the current was 0.05 C. Repeated the above charging and discharging operations three times.

The experimental platform was shown in Fig. S1, including a heating system and a data acquisition system. The battery heating system consisted of an iron frame, a battery fixing device, a thermocouple, a heating rod, and a heating controller. The data acquisition system consisted of thermocouples, data acquisition modules, cameras, and computers. The thermocouple was a K-type armored thermocouple, model WRNK-191, with a diameter of 1 mm and a length of 3 m. The temperature measurement range was 0–1100 °C, and the temperature detection accuracy was ±0.01 °C. The temperature and voltage changes during the thermal runaway process of the battery were collected using thermocouples and recorded using the ICPCON Hongge M-7019 data acquisition module, with an error of within 0.1 s. A Panasonic V180 high-definition camera was used to record macroscopic phenomena.

Before the experiment, the heating rod and the pretreated batteries (0 % SOC, 50 % SOC, and 100 % SOC) were placed together. Then, thermocouples were installed at the outs surfaces of the top, middle, and bottom outs surfaces of the battery to measure the temperature of the middle, top, and bottom of the battery, and record them as Tt, Tm, and Tb respectively. All of them were tied tightly with high temperature resistant strong adhesive tape, and installed on an iron stand. Then, other circuits were connected according to Fig. S1. The heating power of the temperature control box was set to 250 W. Next, computer, camera and the temperature control box were turned on in turn to trigger the experiment. After the safety valve bursts open, the temperature control box was immediately closed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment of suppression of battery thermal runaway by different additive water mist
ce:section.ce:para: Tween-20, Silok®2235, FC-7430 and TX-100 were obtained through commercial purchase. CQDs was prepared according to document [31], the detailed content see Supplement Information S2. The surfactants' concentration ranges were selected near the Critical Micelle Concentration points. CQDs is physical additive, its concentration range was obtained through preliminary screening experiments, from low concentration to high concentration.
The concentration of additives was showed in Table 1 .
The experimental platform was shown in Fig. 1 , which was designed and constructed by ourselves based on the data of thermal runaway of 26650 battery.

Experimental steps for suppressing battery thermal runaway with additive water mist were following. First, the batteries were preprocessed according to Section 2.1 and charged to 100 % SOC. additive aqueous solution was prepared according to Table 1. Second, the fine water mist release system was connected and the pump pressure was adjusted to a certain value. The battery to the heating rod and thermocouple according were bound according to Fig. 1 and Fig. S1 and loaded them onto the battery rack. The thermocouple, temperature control box, data acquisition system, computer and other wiring were next connected in turn. Next, the computer and PLC software were turned on, the system connection was checked. Setting alarm temperature and voltage thresholds, as well as fine water mist release time. Last, pressing the green start button on the control box panel to start the experiment. The PLC turned on and adjusted the heating controller through IO control.

When the heating began, the battery temperature started to rise. The system executed the detection program and control logic program. When the battery temperature and voltage exceeded the threshold, the system automatically disconnected the heater power and alarmed, automatically started the water pump to release fine water mist, and automatically shut down the water pump after a certain period of continuous release. The fine water mist release nozzle was a three-way nozzle, with an inner diameter of 0.3 mm for each nozzle.

In order to minimize the errors caused by batteries and environment, the same batch batteries were choice for experiments under the same temperature and humidity environment. In addition, the release conditions of fine water mist were determined by a pre-experiment of pure fine water mist suppressing thermal runaway of lithium batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal runaway of 26650 batteries with different SOC
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature and voltage variation of 26650 battery thermal runaway process was showed in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that although the battery's charge state was different, their thermal runaway process was similar. The process was as follows.

First, the batteries temperature increased with increasing heating time, simultaneously accompanied by gas overflow. When the temperature raised to a certain value, the voltage suddenly decreased. The possible reason is that the large enough area of electrolyte membrane inside the battery decomposed and ruptured at this temperature [32], leading to internal short circuits in the battery [30,33,34]. Then, the lithium in the negative electrode of the battery was exposed to the electrolyte, triggering a fast side reaction that produced many gases and heat. When the gas pressure exceeded the limit of the battery, the safety valve burst, a lot of gases was ejected. This made the pressure and temperature inside the battery decrease so that the internal short circuit of the battery has been temporarily alleviated and the battery voltage rebound. Next, the battery voltage fluctuated some time, this was because of the over and over again of generation and release of gas and heat inside the battery in such time interval. During this process, the battery temperature continued to rise, and the degree of damage to the electrolyte membrane inside the battery increased gradually. The internal reactions of the battery became more intense, ultimately leading to the complete destruction of the electrode film inside the battery, and the reaction products violently rushed out. At this point, the battery voltage dropped to zero. When the escaping combustible gas concentration reached the ignition limit, they would be ignited by high temperature, and resulting a jet fire. After the combustible material burned out, the battery temperature gradually decreased to room temperature. Jet fire didn't occur for 0 % SOC 26650 battery. The possible reason was the negative electrode of the battery was in a lithium poor state, which resulted in slow reactions, so the gas generated during the thermal runaway process did not reach the lower explosive limit [35].

The main parameters of thermal runway of 26650 LIBs with different SOC were showed in Table 2 . The voltage drop rate was calculated as formula:
(1)
R vd
= ΔV /
t min
−
t 0
here, Rvd was voltage drop rate, V/s; ΔV was the total voltage drop; tmin was the time when the voltage firstly decreased to minimum, s; t0 was the time when the voltage began to decrease, s.

It was concluded according to Fig. 2 and Table 2 that due to the differences in battery manufacturing, there was no rigorous regularity in the time and temperature of voltage drop, safety valve burst, and jet fire occurrence. But overall, under thermal abuse conditions, the general process of thermal runaway of 26650 batteries included five stages. They were temperature rise, voltage drop, safety valve burst, jet fire (or sudden emission of a large amount of gas for 0 SOC), and temperature drop in turn. According to the above analysis, the voltage drops in voltage meant a widespread rupture of the electrolyte membrane inside the battery, leading to irreversible thermal runaway of the battery. So, the occurrence of voltage drop could be seen as the main criterion for determining the occurrence of battery thermal runaway. And temperature could be seen as an auxiliary judgment condition. It could be concluded that the safety valve exploded over 49 s after the voltage drop occurred; jet fire occurred over 156 s later; the average rate of battery voltage decrease is higher than 0.44 V/s; the temperature at which the battery voltage began to decrease is greater than or equal to 116 °C. Based on these data, an intelligent small-scale lithium battery thermal runaway suppression experimental platform was designed and constructed. The detailed information was showed in Supplement Information S3. This experimental platform could also be used to other types of batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement effect of different surfactant additives on the suppression of thermal runaway in 26650 LIBs by fine water mist
ce:section.ce:para: The pressure of the pump that generated fine water mist was 0.3 MPa, which was determined by a series of experimental studies, see Supplement Information S4. The total flow rate of fine water mist was 0.28 L/min. The nozzle was located 30 cm above the battery.

Taking the temperature as the main evaluation parameters and battery cooling rate as an auxiliary evaluation parameter, the enhancement effect of different surfactant additives on the suppression of thermal runaway in 26650 LIBs by fine water mist was evaluated [24]. The temperature variation curves of 26650 lithium-ion battery thermal runaway suppressed by different concentrations of Tween-20, Silok®2235, FC-7430 and TX-100 fine water mist were showed in Fig. 3 . It can be seen that all the colored temperature variation curves were lower than black ones in the same picture which indicated that the addition of any concentration Tween-20, Silok®2235, FC-7430 and TX-100 could enhance the suppression of fine water mist to the thermal runaway in the LIB. The effect of additive concentration on the peak temperature of battery followed a similar pattern, i.e., the peak temperatures of Tt and Tm decreased first and then increased with the increase of Tween-20, Silok®2235, FC-7430 and TX-100 addition. The possible reason was that the addition of surfactant can reduce the surface tension of water solution so reducing the droplet size of water mist [26]. The droplet size decreased with the increase of surfactant concentration. Usually, the smaller the droplet, the easier it was to evaporate. Therefore, the smaller the particle size of fine water mist, the better its cooling effect. But for the cooling of lithium batteries body, the longer the droplets stay on the surface of the battery, the better. The synergistic effect of the two opposite effects resulted in the existence of an optimal concentration of additives. In addition, because different surfactants had different effects on droplet size, the wetting power and the adhesion of the water solution [36], the optimal concentration also varied.

For Tween-20 additive, the he optimal inhibitory effect was achieved at the mass concentration of Tween-20 of 75 ppm, and the peak temperatures of Tt and Tm were 246 °C and 240 °C, respectively. For Silok®2235 addictive, the optimal inhibitory effect was achieved at the mass concentration of Silok®2235 of 600 ppm, and the peak temperatures of Tt and Tm were 242 °C and 239 °C, respectively. For FC-7430 addictive, the optimal inhibitory effect was achieved at the mass concentration of FC-7430 of 200 ppm, and the peak temperatures of Tt and Tm were 259 °C and 256 °C, respectively. For TX-100 addictive, the optimal inhibitory effect was achieved at the mass concentration of TX-100 of 200 ppm, and the peak temperatures of Tt and Tm were 274 °C and 261 °C, respectively.

The optimal addition amounts of four surfactants and their enhanced inhibitory effects on fine water mist are shown in Table 3 . The battery cooling rate were calculated respectively and showed in Table 3. The battery cooling rate was calculated as formula:
(2)
R c
=
T b
− 100 /
t b
−
t 100
here, Rc was battery cooling rate, °C/s; Tb was the peak temperature in the middle of battery, °C; tb was the time of peak temperature in the middle of the battery, s; t100 was the time when Tb drop to 100 °C, s.

It can be seen from Table 3 that although the above four additives were the same type, the enhancement effect of them on the suppression of fine water mist were different. Comparing the peak temperatures of Tt and Tm and the cooling rate, 600 ppm Silok®2235 has the best enhancement effect, followed by 75 ppm Tween-20, 200 ppm FC-7430, and 200 ppm TX-100. This was related to their surface tension and foaming properties.

The addition of surfactants can reduce the surface tension of fine water mist [28]. The surface tension of four fine water mist with optimal concentrations of surfactants was shown in Fig. 4 . As shown in the figure, the addition of surfactant could reduce the surface tension of fine water mist. The lower the surface tension of the liquid, the smaller the particle size of the fine water mist, the faster the evaporation rate of the fine water mist, and the better the cooling and suffocation effects of the fine water mist [37]. The lower the surface tension of the liquid, the better the wetting ability and adhesion of the droplet to the battery [36]. The D90 value of fine water mist drop with the addition of 600 ppm Silok®2235, 75 ppm Tween-20, 200 ppm FC-7430, and 200 ppm TX-100 were showed in Supplement Information S5. It can be seen that all the mist droplet with surfactant were lower than that of pure water, and the smallest particle size was droplet of with 600 ppm Silok®2235 case. Therefore, the addition of surfactants could enhance the suppression of thermal runaway of batteries by fine water mist, and the best enhancement effect was obtained for 600 ppm Silok®2235 water mist.

According to Fig. 4, the foaming rates of the four surfactants water liquid were different. The existence of foam was beneficial to the suppression of oil pool fires and solid combustible fires, but it was disadvantageous to the suppression of battery thermal runaway because the foam prevents the outward transfer of heat generated by the internal reaction of the battery which was just the main reason of battery thermal runaway. So, the lower the foaming rate of surfactant aqueous solution, the better the suppression effect of fine water mist on battery thermal runaway. It can be seen from Fig. 4, both the surface tension and foaming properties of 600 ppm Silok®2235 liquid was low, so 600 ppm Silok®2235's enhancement effect on fine water mist suppression was the best. Although the surface tension of 75 ppm Tween-20 fine water mist was the highest, its foaming ability was the smallest, which made its enhancement effect the second. The surface tension of 200 ppm FC-7430 fine water mist was the lower, but its foaming ability was the highest, so its enhanced effect was the next. The surface tension of 200 ppm TX-100 fine water mist was moderate and the foaming rate was high, so the reinforcing effect of 200 ppm TX-100 on fine water mist was the worst. According to the above results, the foaming performance of fine water mist had a significant impact on the inhibitory effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced effect of CQDs additive on the suppression of thermal runaway in 26650 LIB by fine water mist
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature variation curves of 26650 lithium-ion battery thermal runaway suppressed by different CQDs concentration fine water mist was showed in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that all the colored temperature variation curves were lower than black ones in the same picture which indicated that the addition of 500 ppm to 1400 ppm CQDs could enhance the suppression of fine water mist. The peak temperatures of Tt and Tm decreased first and then increased with the increase of CQDs addition. The optimal inhibitory effect was achieved at the mass concentration of CQDs of 1200 ppm, and the peak temperatures of Tt and Tm were 231 °C and 234 °C, and the Tm cooling rate was 5.05 °C/s. The two peak temperatures were even lower than Tt (242 °C) and Tm (239 °C) of 600 ppm Silok®2235 fine water mist suppression action. This suggested that enhancement effect of 1200 ppm CQDs was better than that of 600 ppm Silok®2235.

The possible reason was analyzed as follows. First, there was plenty of –OH, –COOH groups in the surface of CQDs, Fig. 6 . –OH, –COOH groups are polar functional groups which are prone to adsorb free radicals, which make carbon dots good ability to absorb free radicals in flames and interrupt combustion chain reactions. Second, CQDs would undergo endothermic decomposition above 200 °C according to the TG and DSC curves of CQDs, Fig. 6, which made the CQDs paly a certain cooling effect. Third, when heated, the surface functional groups (–OH, –COOH) on CQDs will undergo esterification or polymerization reactions of intermolecular or intramolecular, producing ester groups and water [38]. If the ester groups continue to be heated, they will react with oxygen in the air to produce carbon dioxide. So newly formed inert substances will temporarily remain in the fire and have a suffocating effect. To sum up, these three effects really exist and all play a role in the enhancement the suppression of thermal runaway in fine water mist. In addition, there is a certain synergy between the three of them. Due to the temperature difference, CQDs entering the fire will absorb heat through thermal radiation and heat transfer from the surrounding area, and does also absorb free radicals. The absorbed free radicals will release heat to CQDs, thereby promoting CQDs' decomposition. The decomposition products can hinder the contact between combustible gases and oxygen, causing combustion to be interrupted. Therefore, CQDs showed good enhancement effect on the suppression of thermal runaway in 26650 LIBs by fine water mist. But, when the concentration of CQDs is too high, it would reduce the amount of water reaching the fire or battery surface, thereby reducing the cooling effect of water. Therefore, 1200 ppm CQDs was the best addition concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced effect of composite additives of surfactants and CQDs on the suppression of thermal runaway in 26650 LIBs by fine water mist
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above analysis, the enhancement mechanism of surfactants and CQDs on the suppression of fine water mist were different. This urged us to investigate the synergistic enhancement effect of their joint use. After all, the better the suppression effect of fine water mist on thermal runaway of LIBs, the more beneficial for the prevention and emergency response of LIB accidents. So, the joint use of 1200 ppm CQDs with 600 ppm Silok®2235 and 75 ppm Tween-20 was studied subsequently.

The temperature variation curves of 26650 lithium-ion battery thermal runaway suppressed by composite additive fine water mist were showed in Supplement Information S6. It can be seen that the Tt and Tm curves of composite additive were lower than that of any single one. This suggested that the combination of surfactants and CQDs could further enhance the inhibitory effect of fine water mist. The peak temperatures of Tt and Tm of thermal runaway of battery and battery cooling rate under the action of composite additive fine water mist were showed in Table 4 . Under the suppression of 600 ppm Silok®2235 and 1200 ppm CQDs composite additive fine water mist, the highest temperature of Tt and Tm were 212 °C and 216 °C, which were respectively lower than that of under the suppression of either 600 ppm Silok®2235 additive fine water mist or 1200 ppm CQDs additive fine water mist. Comparing the data in the Table 4, composite additive of Silok®2235 (600 ppm) & CQDs (1200 ppm) was the best, which was also better than many reported additives [11,20,22].

It can be seen from Fig. 7 that the composite additive had a very significant strengthening effect on the suppression of fine water mist. Compared with the case of no fine water mist, Silok®2235 (600 ppm) & CQDs (1200 ppm) composite additive water mist made peak Tt and Tm reduce 408 °C and 318 °C respectively, and the battery cooling rate increase about 44 times. Compared with pure fine water mist, the addition of Silok®2235 (600 ppm) & CQDs (1200 ppm) composite additive made peak Tt and Tm reduce 116 and 108 °C respectively, and the battery cooling rate increase 1.67 times. The enhancement effect of joint use of Silok®2235 & CQDs additive was better than that of any single use.

To sum up, the optimized Silok®2235 & CQDs composite additive fine water mist was an efficient and environmentally friendly lithium battery thermal runaway suppression/fire extinguishing agent, this will improve the prevention and emergency response capabilities for lithium-ion battery thermal runaway accidents.

In this paper, an efficient and environmentally friendly composite additive fine water mist was developed and evaluated. First, the thermal runaway of 26650 LIB under thermal abuse condition were carefully studied. It was found the thermal runaway included five stages, i.e., temperature rise, voltage drop, safety valve rupture, jet fire, and temperature drop. The voltage drop of the battery was a key factor in determining whether irreversible thermal runaway has occurred. Based on the data, an intelligent small-scale lithium battery thermal runaway suppression experimental platform was designed and constructed. Second, the inhibitory effect of five single additive fine water mist or their composite additive fine water mist on thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries was studied using this platform. The enhancement effect of additives on the inhibition of fine water mist, from strong to weak, was as follows: CQDs, Silok®2235, Tween-20, FC-7430 and TX-100. The enhancement effect of surfactants was related to the surface tension and foaming properties of their mixed solution, that was, the lower the surface tension and foaming rate, the better the enhancement effect. The enhancement effect of SQDs on the inhibition of fine water mist was attributed to quench free radicals' ability, the cooling and suffocating effect. The joint use of surfactant and SQDs showed synergistic effect. Last, an efficient and environmentally friendly composite additive fine water mist for the suppression of thermal runaway of LIBs, i.e., 600 ppm Silok®2235 & 1200 ppm CQDs composite additive fine water mist was obtained. The research results provide an efficient inhibitor for suppressing thermal runaway in lithium batteries.

Lixia Li: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Yilin Zhang: Software, Data curation. Qingchen Li: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Wang Zhan: Methodology, Investigation. Zhen Chen: Supervision, Project administration. Mingyi Chen: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Cheng Yuhe: Visualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper named” Efficient and environmentally friendly composite additive fine water mist for suppressing thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was sponsored by Special project for the construction of the Key Emergency Management School of Jiangsu University (JG-04-07), and Zhenjiang Science and Technology Plan Social Development Project (ZK20240533), and the Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Material of Water Treatment, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 215009.
